blocks.body: Common Mistakes helps you notice frequent problems with articles, word order, the Present Perfect, quantity, prepositions, and typical B1/B2 traps.

{"level":"A2-B2","intro":"In this branch you do not just review a rule. You learn to spot recurring breakdowns in full sentences and turn them into natural English more quickly.","rules":[{"label":"SPOT THE PATTERN","color":"emerald","text":"Each lesson focuses on short, repeatable mistakes that often come from translation habits or automatic first guesses.","example":"She is teacher. -> She is a teacher."},{"label":"COMPARE WRONG VS RIGHT","color":"blue","text":"It is not enough to know the rule. You need to see the wrong version next to the corrected one and understand why one sounds natural and the other does not.","example":"I have seen him yesterday. -> I saw him yesterday."},{"label":"BUILD A CHECKING HABIT","color":"amber","text":"At the end of each lesson, use the quick takeaways as a self-edit checklist for writing and speaking.","example":"Check: article, word order, tense, noun type, preposition."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. What This Section Includes
blocks.body:
The opening Common Mistakes branch stays flat and clear: one category and six direct lessons in a logical sequence.

  1. Articles: Common Mistakes - how not to lose a / an / the and when to leave the zero article.
  2. Word Order: Common Mistakes - typical problems with SVO, adverb position, questions, and negatives.
  3. Present Perfect: Common Mistakes - finished time, been / gone, for / since, yet / already / just.
  4. Countable vs Uncountable Nouns: Common Mistakes - many / much, few / little, advice / information / homework.
  5. Prepositions: Common Mistakes - in / on / at, dependent prepositions, and movement prepositions.
  6. B1/B2 Grammar Traps - compact traps with tenses, modal structures, inversion, conditionals, and linkers.

{"title":"2. How to Use This Branch","items":["Start by deciding where the mistake lives: an article, word order, tense, noun type, preposition, or a higher-level B1/B2 pattern.","Do not correct a sentence blindly. Compare the wrong pattern with the corrected version, then check the explanation.","Watch for signals: finished time, a singular countable noun, a fixed preposition, an auxiliary in a question, or a general meaning.","After each lesson, keep one short checking rule you can reuse in a real email, message, or classroom answer."]}
